# Verify the --dry-run, --quiet, and --aggressive flags on `sesam clean`.
#
# Default `sesam clean` loads the Repo and removes only sesam-known revealed
# plaintexts (the paths recorded in the audit log; here just README.md after
# init). `sesam clean --aggressive` skips the Repo load and instead walks the
# worktree, removing every untracked file (still skipping `.sesam/`).

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# README.md is the only revealed plaintext sesam knows about after init.
exists README.md

# --- scenario 1: --dry-run prints the known plaintext but does not delete it.
exec sesam clean --dry-run
stdout 'README\.md'
exists README.md

# --- scenario 2: --quiet stays silent and deletes the known plaintext.
exec sesam clean --quiet
! stdout '.+'
! exists README.md

# An arbitrary untracked file in the worktree is invisible to the default
# (soft) clean because sesam only acts on paths it has recorded.
cp leaked.txt leaked.txt
exec sesam reveal
exec sesam clean
exists leaked.txt

# --- scenario 3: --aggressive walks the worktree and wipes leaked.txt too,
#                 still skipping the contents of `.sesam/`.
mkdir -p .sesam/tmp/scratch
exec cp leaked.txt .sesam/tmp/scratch/spill
exec sesam clean --aggressive --quiet
! exists leaked.txt
exists .sesam/tmp/scratch/spill

-- leaked.txt --
leaked plaintext
